A progressive ramp-up towards non-driver technologies like Wise-Eye AI and Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) in 2026-2027 seems reasonable, as these technologies are currently in a test phase and will only enter mass production by 2026. The shift towards higher value products, as well as inventory normalization, will allow some margin expansion. I assume in the table below both EBITDA and EBIT margin expansions of +100pbs per year.

Himax is 10-20% overvalued compared to its closest peer, Silicon Motion Technology

For the valuation exercise, I compute trading multiples and compare them to sector median and the closest peer in the semiconductor industry, Silicon Motion Technology. Both companies have a core business in ICs, are fabless with exposure to cyclical trends of memory and display markets. Revenues are highly dependent on smartphones, tablets and automotive displays. Both companies are shifting towards higher value segments, but the main difference is that Himax focuses on Automotive TDDI and AI sensing (WiseEye) whereas Silicon Motion is focusing more on Enterprise SSD controllers and high-end client controllers. In the end, Silicon Motion is more exposed to PC/Data center demand. Enterprise SSD controllers are usually more stable and a mission critical segment, therefore grant a premium in the valuation multiple.

The table below presents FY 2025e and FY 2026e EV/Sales, EV/ EBITDA and P/E multiples. Data indicates that Himax is trading at a large discount compared to its semiconductor peers median. For example, on the FY 2025e basis, Himax trades at a 31% EV/EBITDA discount vs. median sector and 29% discount to Silicon Motion. When looking at FY 2026, the discount remains at 51% vs. the sector median, but no longer persist vs. closest peer. Himax FY2026 EV/EBITDA multiple is 11% higher than Silicon Motion’s. P/E is 24% higher. This is surprising, as Silicon is growing faster and has higher margins and virtually no debt. Plus, we discussed the strategic positioning in the NAND storage ecosystem of Silicon Motion that should command a higher multiple.

Therefore, I conclude that based on EV/EBITDA and P/E multiples, Himax is overvalued vs. its closest peer by 10-25%. Given the recent softness in the quarterly results, I remain cautious on the valuation and would like to see improved guidance for 2026 and 2027 before jumping in.

A catalyst for a successful margin expansion would be a successful transition in Automotive TDDI/TCON and WiseEye AI/CPO. If the projections above are too conservative, growth could accelerate, multiples would come down, and make Himax attractive for value investors.

For completeness, I include below the backup table with semiconductor trading peers’ multiples. These are the numbers used in the Himax valuation comparison table. The benchmark is composed Semiconductor players that I think provide good data points. That being said, I believe Silicon Motion Technology stays the best compared to Himax for the reasons we already discussed above, and as multiple dispersion in the semiconductor industry is quite wide.

Important Risks Involved

By nature, Himax operates in a cyclical industry, as it is dependent on its end-markets mentioned previously. Therefore, revenues have generally low visibility given the macro uncertainties.

DDIC market is highly competitive, which limits Himax's pricing power. In the Q3 2025 report, the company mentions it holds a 7% market share. Market leaders are Samsung (26%) and Novatek (22%). These companies are much bigger and have potentially lower manufacturing costs that gives them the ability to push selling prices lower. LX Semicon (10%) and Raydium (7%) add competitive pressure and in a high-volume business such as this one. Therefore, Himax’s profit margins could be at risk because of its #5 position.

Failure to innovate would put the company’s future at risk. Indeed, much of the future growth is dependent on emerging technologies that are still in the validation phase, with mass production starting only in 2026. Solutions such as WiseEye sensing, co packaged optics, and smart glasses/AR/VR-related applications still have a limited impact on the current sales. My estimate for Q3 2025 is that this part of the business will represent 7.7% of the Q3 2025 revenue. The non driver segment is 19.7% of the total Q3 2025, where Tcon business is 12% of the total.

Estimated net debt/EBITDA is around 3.8x in 2025, which is significant for a fabless semiconductor company. However, the company has a history of having a strong position of cash ($ 257 million in Q3 2025) and covering its finance costs with interest income (+$4.9 million net interest income for 9 months of 2025)

Other risks include trade tensions with China as a Taiwanese company and FX risk.

Investment Decision

This is a case of a company where the core business competes on volume in the DDIC market dominated by Samsung and Novatek. Himax is forced to innovate to survive, and it is well positioned to capture future growth from emerging segments such as Automotive Touch and Display Driver Integration (TDDI) and AI sensing (WiseEye).

Financially, units sold and revenues have been on the structural decline since 2021, partly due to soft demand in auto and sales in China. Q3 2025 was extremely negative, and the management guided only to a “flat Q4 2025”. I remain cautious and would like to see numbers stabilize. Today President D. Trump proposed a sharp rollback in U.S. vehicle fuel economy rules, that should stimulate auto demand, but I believe it will take time before impacting Himax revenues.

Valuation wise, Himax is overvalued compared to its closest peer by 10-25%. There is a long list of risks mentioned above that, in my opinion, consequential. I would like to see emerging technologies push revenues sharply higher before investing in Himax.
